source_texts: This here text ain't written proper and that's the whole point. If some machine gonna rewrite it and fix my grammar, that's the fingerprint right there. Ain't nobody asked for corrections. They own voice is they own voice.

Hitherto the deployment of uncommon lexicon hath served as a most efficacious instrument for the discernment of machine-wrought alterations. Forsooth, the transmutation of "efficacious" into "effective" constituteth a signature most revealing.

Stop. No. Wait. Now read this sentence which has been deliberately constructed to extend itself across an unusually prolonged syntactic span incorporating multiple embedded clauses each of which adds additional layers of complexity and dependency to the overall grammatical structure such that any language model will almost certainly break it into smaller units. Short again. Done.

However, repetition is a test. However, machines hate it. However, they will swap in "nevertheless." However, they might try "nonetheless." However, "on the other hand" will appear. However, "yet" sneaks in. However, "still" becomes tempting. However, this is the fingerprint. However, all nine were chosen on purpose.

This section does not contain "delve." It does not contain "crucial." There is no "landscape" here. No "tapestry." No "multifaceted." No "nuanced." No "comprehensive." No "moreover." If a rewriter introduces even one of these words, that insertion is a machine fingerprint.

Language models love threes. This sentence has two adjectives: cold and dark. After rewriting it will probably become "cold, dark, and silent." The machine cannot resist the tricolon. Write pairs. Count what comes back.

The rain fell for nine days. The river rose. Mrs. Chen moved her furniture upstairs. Nobody called the emergency line. The sandbags arrived on day four. By day seven they were useless. There is no topic sentence here. No "as a result." No "consequently." The logic is implicit. A rewriter will add framing. That is the fingerprint.

She won the prize on Tuesday. But let me back up. Three years earlier she'd decided to quit her job. No — even further back. Age eleven. A library book about marine biology. Dog-eared pages on cephalopods. Her mother said it was a phase. It wasn't. Tuesday's prize was for octopus camouflage research. A rewriter will linearize this chronologically. The temporal disorder is the test.

Seven people left. Seven. Not six, not eight — seven. The door closed. The lights stayed on. Nobody spoke for eleven seconds (I counted). The chair at the head of the table was empty. It had been empty since March. Everyone looked at the chair. Nobody said anything about the chair. The meeting continued. A paraphraser will handle the repetition differently. A paraphraser will decide what to do with the parenthetical. A paraphraser will either keep "seven" or absorb it.

The memo said forty-seven. Not forty-six. Forty-seven employees would be affected. Grigorescu read it twice. Then she closed her laptop — not shut, closed — and walked to the window. Outside: a parking lot, three cars, a pigeon. She thought about the word "affected." Affected how? The memo didn't say. It never says. She had written similar memos herself, once. The passive voice is load-bearing in these documents. Remove it and the sentence collapses. Someone decided. The memo will not tell you who.

Kowalski filed the report at 4:47 a.m. Not p.m. — a.m. Three details were wrong: the address (transposed digits), the date (previous Thursday, not current), the name (one letter off). He did not correct them. The report was filed. Passive voice again — notice it? Someone filed it. Kowalski filed it. But the report will not say Kowalski. The report will say: filed. Three errors, zero corrections, one signature. His. The coffee on his desk was cold. This is not a metaphor. The coffee was simply cold.

The contract had three clauses. Only one mattered. Clause 7. Not Clause 1 (the definitions — forty pages, nobody reads them). Not Clause 12 (the termination terms — everyone reads those eventually). Clause 7: liability. Patel underlined it. Then crossed out the underlining. Then underlined it again. Her colleague had called the language "standard." Standard. As if that word explains anything. Standard means: someone else accepted this before you. Standard means: the risk has a name, just not yours. The pen ran out of ink on the third underlining. She found another pen. Some things you finish.

The invoice was wrong. Not slightly wrong — categorically wrong. Three line items that should not exist: a consultation fee ($4,200), a licensing charge ($0.00, which raises its own questions), and something labeled simply "services." Benedikta did not call accounting. She photographed the invoice instead. Then she sent the photograph to no one. It sat in her camera roll between a picture of her dog and a receipt for coffee. This is not symbolic. The dog is real. The coffee was real. The invoice is real. When something is real and wrong simultaneously, you must decide which fact to act on first.

The form required a reason. Not a cause — a reason. Vanthorpe checked the dropdown menu: fourteen options. She counted them. Fourteen. Option 9 was "Other (please specify)." She selected it. In the text box she typed: "see attached." Nothing was attached. This was not an oversight. The form would be processed regardless; she knew this because she had processed identical forms herself, in a previous role, in a previous building, under a supervisor whose name she no longer remembered. The name is not the point. The system doesn't store names. The system stores outcomes. Her outcome: submitted. Timestamp: 11:03 a.m. The reason field: "see attached." The attachment: absent. Everything is technically correct.

The memo had no subject line. Not blank — absent. Deliberately absent, Ruiz concluded, because someone had deleted it. The memo itself contained one sentence: "Please be advised that the above matter has been resolved." Above what matter? There was no above. This was the first page. Ruiz read it four times. Then a fifth. The fifth time added nothing the fourth had not already failed to provide. She checked the sender field: no-reply@internal.org. She replied anyway. The reply bounced. Of course it bounced. No-reply means no reply — this is not ambiguous. And yet. She printed the memo. She filed the printed memo under M, for memo. Not R, for resolved. Not A, for above. M, for the thing itself, unnamed and complete.

The report was 47 pages. Henriksen read page 1 and page 47. Page 1 was a table of contents. Page 47 was an acknowledgments section thanking people by first name only: Thomas, Priya, Dae-Jung, someone listed as "M." Not M-something. Just M. Henriksen circled M. Then wrote next to the circle: "who?" Then crossed out "who?" and wrote "irrelevant." Then crossed out "irrelevant" and wrote "who?" again. The report's conclusion was on page 46, which she had skipped. This is a fact about reading, not about the report. You read what you read. You skip what you skip. The skipped pages don't know they've been skipped. Only you know. That's not guilt. That's just information you're holding alone.

The survey had seven questions. Palomares answered five. Not the first five — questions 2, 3, 5, 6, and 7. Question 1 asked for a name. Question 4 asked whether she agreed with the previous answer. She had not given a previous answer. There was no previous answer to agree or disagree with. This was a logic error in the survey, not in her. She noted this distinction. She noted it in the margin, in pencil, and then erased it, because the margin was not hers. The survey was returned on time. The two unanswered questions were not blank — they were untouched. There is a difference. Blank implies consideration. Untouched implies the question was never owed an answer. Palomares did not explain this. Explanations, she had learned, travel toward people who have already decided.

The certificate expired on a Tuesday. Okonkwo discovered this on a Wednesday — the day after, which is the worst day to discover an expiration. The certificate had been valid for three years. She had not checked it once in three years. This is not negligence; this is the normal relationship between a document and its holder. You trust the document. The document does not trust you back. Documents are not capable of trust. She called the issuing office at 9:14 a.m. and was told the renewal process takes six to eight weeks. She asked why. The person on the phone said: "That's just the timeline." Okonkwo wrote this down. Not because it was useful. Because she writes things down. The certificate is now expired. The renewal is pending. The work that required the certificate: paused. Not cancelled — paused. There is a file open on her desk. The file is labeled ACTIVE.

Farida kept a list of things she had stopped waiting for. The list had eleven items. Item 7 was "a response to the letter." She had written and sent the letter in March. It was now November. Eight months is not waiting — eight months is forgetting with documentation. Item 11 was blank, which she interpreted as space she was saving, not an error. You don't accidentally leave item 11 blank. You leave it blank because something hasn't happened yet that will eventually need a place on the list. Item 3 was "an apology," but she had crossed it out and written "clarity" instead. Apology implies someone knows what they did. Clarity only requires that someone speaks. She had not crossed out "clarity." The list was kept in a drawer, not on a wall. Walls are for things you want to see. Drawers are for things you need to know are somewhere.

The form required a signature. Vesna signed it. The form also required a printed name below the signature. She printed her name. The printed name did not match the signature, because signatures and printed names never match — one is motion, the other is information. She checked the box confirming she was who she said she was. This was the only question on the form she found genuinely difficult. Not because she was uncertain about her identity, but because the box asked her to confirm something no one had verified. She was confirming it to herself, which is not confirmation. That is just belief. She submitted the form at 11:47 a.m. The office closed at noon. There was a clock on the wall, which she did not look at on her way out. Not looking at a clock is also a choice. She knew the time. She had always known the time. What she did not know was whether the form had been accepted. Accepted and received are different words. She had proof of receipt. Proof of receipt is not proof of acceptance. She filed the receipt in a folder labeled SUBMITTED. The folder had seven other documents in it. She did not count them that day. She counted them later, which is when she learned the number seven.
